Catastrophe Modeling Research

Climate-Conditioned Cyclone Frequency Pipeline

A Bayesian framework linking satellite-observed SST anomalies to tropical cyclone genesis rates across four global basins, validated against landmark storms Otis, Freddy, Harvey, and Biparjoy.

Basins Studied 4 Global Basins
Temporal Coverage 1982 – 2025
Data Sources NOAA OISST · ERA5-Land · IBTrACS
Platform GEE · Databricks · PyMC
Objective Methodology Basins Results Storm Frequency SST Correlation Hazard Scaling Key Findings Next Steps
Section 01

Research Objective

Build a working prototype that ingests global climate reanalysis data, extracts localized ocean and atmospheric features, and uses Bayesian inference to output a near-term climate-conditioned storm frequency rate (λ) for specific ocean basins.

The pipeline is validated against four landmark tropical cyclones whose extreme intensity has been attributed to climate change, and then tested on the question: did the climate signal persist after these storms, and did subsequent storms follow?

λ
Poisson Rate Parameter

The core output — a dynamically updated, SST-conditioned expected storm count per basin per year, replacing static historical averages.

+0.5–0.8°C
SST Anomaly Signal

All four landmark storms formed over ocean basins with SST anomalies of 0.5–0.8°C above the 1982–2020 climatological baseline.

43 yr
Baseline Record

NOAA OISST satellite-era SST record from 1982 to 2025, providing consistent ocean temperature measurements across all basins.

Section 02

Methodology & Pipeline Architecture

The pipeline is structured in four phases, from cloud-based spatial data wrangling through Bayesian posterior estimation. Each phase feeds the next with clean, basin-specific climate covariates and storm frequency observations.

Phase 1

Spatial Ingestion

Google Earth Engine: NOAA OISST (ocean SST) + ERA5-Land (atmospheric) filtered by basin ROI, aggregated monthly, exported as CSV.

Phase 1B

Storm Data

IBTrACS v04r01: parsed per basin, filtered to ROI, annual counts by intensity category (TS, Cat 1-2, Cat 3+).

Phase 2

Feature Engineering

Seasonal SST anomaly, dewpoint depression, wind speed, precipitation merged with annual storm counts. Genesis thresholds identified.

Phase 3

Bayesian Engine

Negative Binomial GLM via PyMC: log(μ) = β₀ + β₁·SST_anom + β₂·dewpoint. MCMC sampling for posterior λ with credible intervals.

Data Sources

DatasetProviderResolutionCoverageVariables
NOAA OISST V2.1NOAA NCEI0.25° daily1982 – 2025SST (°C), pre-computed anomaly
ERA5-Land MonthlyECMWF via GEE0.1° monthly1982 – 20262m temp, dewpoint, wind (u/v), pressure, precipitation
IBTrACS v04r01NOAA NCEI6-hourly tracks1842 – 2025Lat/lon, wind speed, pressure, storm name/basin
Section 03

Study Basins & Landmark Storms

Four basins were selected, each anchored by a landmark tropical cyclone whose intensity has been linked to anthropogenic ocean warming. The basins span the tropics and represent distinct cyclogenesis regimes.

BasinLandmark StormYearPeak CategorySST AnomalyBaseline SSTPost-Storm Anomaly
Eastern PacificHurricane Otis2023 Category 5 +0.51°C27.31°C+0.41°C
South Indian OceanCyclone Freddy2023 Category 5-eq. +0.55°C26.10°C+0.70°C
Gulf of MexicoHurricane Harvey2017 Category 4 +0.69°C26.26°C+0.73°C
Arabian SeaCyclone Biparjoy2023 Category 3-eq. +0.77°C27.63°C+0.55°C
Section 04

Climate Covariate Results

Phase 1 extracted monthly SST and atmospheric variables for each basin from 1982 to 2025. The table below summarizes the storm-year conditions alongside post-storm persistence — a critical input for the Bayesian frequency model.

Basin (Storm)SST AnomalyWind SpeedDewpoint DepressionSurface PressurePost-Storm SST Trend
E. Pacific (Otis)+0.51°C0.79 m/s7.10°C895 hPa ↓ Slightly declining
S. Indian (Freddy)+0.55°C1.47 m/s6.98°C941 hPa ↑ Increasing (+0.70°C)
Gulf of Mexico (Harvey)+0.69°C1.34 m/s5.23°C1001 hPa ↑ Strongly increasing (+1.0–1.3°C by 2023)
Arabian Sea (Biparjoy)+0.77°C1.94 m/s13.18°C967 hPa ↓ Moderately declining
Dewpoint depression as a discriminator: Harvey's basin (5.2°C) was the most humid — consistent with its catastrophic rainfall. Biparjoy's basin (13.2°C) was the driest, explaining shorter storm duration. Dewpoint depression below ~7°C combined with SST anomaly above +0.5°C represents the highest-probability genesis window.
Section 05

Storm Frequency Analysis

IBTrACS data was filtered to storms passing through each basin's ROI, then categorized by intensity. The stacked bar charts below show the temporal evolution of storm frequency alongside 5-year running means and landmark storm years.

Figure 1
Annual Storm Frequency by Basin (1982–2025)
Stacked bars show tropical storms (blue), Category 1-2 hurricanes (orange), and major hurricanes Cat 3+ (red). The black line traces the 5-year running mean. Dashed vertical lines mark landmark storm years. The Gulf of Mexico panel clearly shows the post-2017 acceleration: after a relative lull in the early 2010s, storm counts surge with major hurricanes appearing consistently from 2017 onward. The Arabian Sea panel shows the recent uptick in a historically quiet basin.
Section 06

SST–Storm Frequency Correlation

The scatter plots below test the central hypothesis: does SST anomaly predict storm frequency? Each point is one year, colored by observation date (dark purple = 1982, bright yellow = 2025). Landmark storms are marked with red stars.

Figure 2
SST Anomaly vs Storm Frequency — Basic Scatter
R² values are modest (0.01–0.06), indicating SST anomaly alone is a weak predictor of total storm count at the annual level. This is physically expected: SST provides the thermodynamic fuel, but wind shear, humidity, and large-scale circulation patterns (ENSO, MJO) determine whether that fuel ignites into organized convection. The Arabian Sea (R²=0.063, p=0.10) shows the strongest linear signal — consistent with it being a basin where SST is the primary limiting factor.
Figure 3
SST Anomaly vs Storm Frequency — With Temporal Colorbar
The colorbar reveals a critical pattern: recent years (yellow/green dots) cluster at higher SST anomalies. In the Gulf of Mexico, the rightward drift of recent observations demonstrates the secular warming trend. The landmark storms sit at the warm end of their basin's distribution — Harvey at +0.69°C, Biparjoy at +0.77°C. The weak R² doesn't mean SST is irrelevant; it means the relationship is nonlinear and modulated by other covariates — exactly what the Bayesian model addresses.
Section 07

Thermodynamic Hazard Scaling

Beyond storm frequency, SST anomalies influence the downstream hazard footprint — precipitation intensity and wind speeds. These relationships are examined across all four basins with storm count as a color dimension.

Figure 4
Hazard Validation: Storm Frequency vs Precipitation & Wind Speed
Left column: storm count vs total annual precipitation. Right column: storm count vs mean coastal wind speed. Most correlations are weak (R < 0.2), which is expected since these are basin-wide annual averages — individual storm rainfall extremes (e.g., Harvey's 1.5m over Houston) are washed out at this scale. The Arabian Sea shows a notable negative wind-storm correlation (R = -0.38, p = 0.011), suggesting that lower ambient wind speeds (less shear) enable cyclogenesis.
Figure 5
SST Anomaly vs Precipitation & Wind — Storm Count Overlay
The Arabian Sea stands out with the strongest SST-precipitation link (R = 0.50, p = 0.001) and SST-wind anticorrelation (R = -0.53, p = 0.000). Warmer Arabian Sea waters produce more precipitation and less surface wind shear — a compounding vulnerability signal. The darker red points (higher storm counts) cluster toward the warm, wet, low-wind corner, exactly where cyclogenesis conditions are most favorable.
Figure 6
Absolute SST vs Hazard Footprints — Raw Temperature Baselines
Using absolute SST instead of anomaly confirms basin-specific thermodynamic baselines. The Arabian Sea operates at the highest absolute SSTs (27–28.5°C) and shows the clearest hazard scaling. Gulf of Mexico SSTs cluster tightly (25.8–27.3°C), explaining why the anomaly — not the absolute value — is the better predictor there: the basin is always warm enough for cyclogenesis, so the anomaly captures the marginal signal.
Section 08

Key Findings

1. Universal warm-water signal: All four landmark storms formed over SST anomalies of +0.5 to +0.8°C above the basin-specific climatological baseline, confirming the thermodynamic prerequisite for extreme cyclone intensification.
2. Gulf of Mexico acceleration: After a 12-year major hurricane drought (2005–2016), 10 major hurricanes made landfall on the Gulf Coast in just 8 years (2017–2024). SST anomalies rose from near-zero to +1.0–1.3°C over this period — the strongest climate-activity coupling in the study.
3. Arabian Sea emergence: Historically the quietest basin, the Arabian Sea shows the strongest statistical SST-storm correlation (R² = 0.06, p = 0.10 for frequency; R = 0.50, p = 0.001 for precipitation). This basin is crossing into a new cyclogenesis regime as SST anomalies reach +0.77°C.
4. Dewpoint depression as the key atmospheric moderator: SST alone has low R² for storm count prediction. The dewpoint depression (T − Tᵈ) discriminates storm environments: basins with depression < 7°C during the storm year (Harvey, Freddy) produced the most devastating rainfall, while Biparjoy's 13.2°C depression limited moisture availability.
5. Wind shear as the suppression signal: The Arabian Sea shows a significant negative correlation between surface wind speed and storm count (R = -0.38, p = 0.011). In years when ambient wind speeds drop, cyclogenesis increases — the warm water can finally organize without being torn apart by shear.
6. Post-storm persistence: In the Gulf of Mexico and South Indian Ocean, SST anomalies increased after the landmark storms, and subsequent major storms followed. This validates the pipeline's core premise: elevated SST is not a one-off condition but a persistent, worsening risk driver.
Section 09

Next Steps & Operational Integration

Real-Time SST Monitoring

Connect daily OISST feed via NOAA ERDDAP. When basin SST exceeds the genesis threshold identified in this pipeline, trigger automated alerts to the underwriting team.

Upper-Level Wind Shear

Add ERA5 pressure-level data (200–850 hPa wind shear) from Copernicus CDS. This is the primary atmospheric suppressor not available on GEE and critical for explaining false-positive warm-SST years.

CMIP6 Forward Projections

Feed bias-corrected CMIP6 SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 projections into the Bayesian engine to produce 1–5 year forward λ estimates for each basin.

Agentic Automation

Wrap the monthly update cycle (pull new ERA5 → recompute anomalies → re-sample posterior → log λ) in a Python agent that runs autonomously at month-end.

Climate-Conditioned Cyclone Frequency Pipeline · Built with Google Earth Engine, NOAA OISST, ERA5-Land, IBTrACS, PyMC

Data period: 1982–2025 · Analysis conducted May 2026
